ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Mutton Bhuna Set; closest match is a generic mutton dish node. The set meal includes garlic naan, rice, and salad components.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mutton, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this spiced mutton curry set meal
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Mutton Bhuna Set. A0CKP (cooked mutton) is used as the primary anchor. The set meal includes garlic naan, rice, and salad which are not captured in this single code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Mutton Bhuna Set or equivalent composite Indian set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 450 g comprising mutton bhuna curry (~200 g), one garlic naan (~90 g), steamed white rice (~120 g), and a small side salad (~40 g).

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Bhuna Set — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Bhuna Set (cr_id G00010663). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Fields not reported by HPB (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, water) are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed Indian curry set meal with white rice and naan
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Bhuna Set as a composite dish. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate components: white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) and plain naan (GI ~71, University of Sydney GI Database). The mutton bhuna curry component contributes protein and fat which attenuate the overall glycaemic response. A blended weighted GI of approximately 62 is defensible for this set meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (62) × available carbohydrate per serving (33.5 g from 450 g × 7.44 g/100g) / 100 = 20.8, rounded to 20.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100 g; dietary fibre only 0.3 g/100 g — insufficient to rescue borderline sodium level
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this set meal and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium content of 467 mg/100 g (approximately 2,100 mg per full serving) substantially exceeds the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold, and the low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) cannot offset the sodium burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Composite high-GI carbohydrate load from white rice (GI ~72) and garlic naan (GI ~71) elevates postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice and reduce the naan portion, as the combined high-GI carbohydrate components of this set meal produce a glycaemic load of approximately 20.7 per serving, which may challenge postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 85 mg/100 g and fat 11.58 g/100 g from red meat (mutton); sodium 467 mg/100 g adds cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this set meal due to the combination of elevated cholesterol (85 mg/100 g), total fat (11.58 g/100 g) from red mutton meat, and high sodium (467 mg/100 g), all of which contribute to atherogenic and hypertensive cardiovascular risk; portion control and reduced-salt preparation are strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (red meat) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory pathways; trace trans fat likely present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish as mutton is a significant source of arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory eicosanoid production; substituting mutton with chicken or oily fish would substantially reduce the pro-inflammatory load while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefit of turmeric and ginger in the spice blend.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Bhuna Set is a complete Indian set meal served in Singapore, comprising mutton bhuna curry, garlic naan, steamed white rice, and a side salad. Bhuna is a dry-frying technique in which spices — typically cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, ginger, and garlic — are cooked in oil until fragrant before mutton pieces are added and braised until the sauce is thick and clinging to the meat with minimal residual liquid. The result is an intensely spiced, richly flavoured curry with tender mutton and a deep, caramelised spice coating. The garlic naan is a leavened flatbread baked in a tandoor or oven, soft and slightly charred, with a pronounced garlic aroma. The set meal provides a balanced combination of protein (14.05 g/100 g), carbohydrate (7.44 g/100 g), and fat (11.58 g/100 g) at 192 kcal/100 g. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg/100 g, making this a moderate-to-high sodium meal. Cholesterol is 85 mg/100 g from the mutton. Dietary fibre is low at 0.3 g/100 g. The dish is popular in Singapore's Indian Muslim and North Indian restaurant segments and is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for hypertension, the composite high-GI carbohydrate load (white rice and naan) is a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management, and the red meat cholesterol and fat profile warrants caution for cardiovascular disease patients. The anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provide partial mitigation but are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from mutton for rheumatoid arthritis patients.
common_user_queries: Is mutton bhuna set good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010663) with confidence 0.95. The HPB record is an approved primary source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for white rice and naan, which are the dominant carbohydrate components; this is documented transparently.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this composite set meal are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010663 as of 2026-04-16. The input record noted a trans fat value of approximately 0.1 g/100 g in the health_suitability section; however, this was not present in the per_100g data block and is therefore set to -1 pending confirmation from an approved source. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Mutton Bhuna Set as a composite dish has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. Serving size of 450 g is an estimated standard set meal portion; HPB did not specify a portion weight for this record. Halal status is assumed based on the Indian Muslim restaurant context in Singapore; formal halal certification should be verified at point of service.
